questions.question: A sealed Ir-192 source is described as 'doubly encapsulated.' What does that mean physically?
options: It contains two different isotopes
The active material is sealed inside two independent welded capsules
It is rated for twice the normal activity
It can be exposed in two directions at once
questions.explanation: Double encapsulation means the active pellet sits inside an inner welded capsule that is itself sealed inside an outer welded capsule, giving two walls of containment.

questions.question: An Ir-192 source starts at 80 Ci. With a half-life of about 74 days, approximately what activity remains after 148 days?
options: 40 Ci
20 Ci
10 Ci
60 Ci
questions.explanation: 148 days is two half-lives (2 x 74). Halving twice: 80 -> 40 -> 20 Ci. Using A = A0 x (1/2)^(t/T-half).

questions.question: Neutron radiography is uniquely suited to imaging which of the following?
options: Cracks in thick steel plate
Low-density materials such as plastics, adhesives, or explosives, even inside metal
Surface scratches on polished metal
The grain structure of cast iron
questions.explanation: Neutrons interact differently from gamma rays and readily reveal low-density, hydrogen-rich materials that conventional radiography cannot, even when surrounded by metal.

questions.question: In an industrial X-ray tube, raising the kVp while holding mA and time constant will primarily:
options: Increase the number of electrons leaving the filament
Increase the penetrating quality (energy) of the X-rays and widen latitude
Lower the heat load on the anode to zero
Have no effect on the beam
questions.explanation: kVp sets the accelerating potential and thus X-ray energy/penetration and the contrast-latitude balance; mA (filament heating) controls electron quantity, not energy.

questions.question: Why must the high voltage feeding an X-ray tube be rectified?
options: To filter soft X-rays from the beam
To convert the transformer's alternating output into the one-directional potential the tube needs
To rotate the anode
To reduce the kVp automatically
questions.explanation: The tube conducts only with the cathode negative and anode positive, so rectifiers (one-way valves) turn AC into DC; constant-potential designs further smooth it for steady output.

questions.question: The line-focus principle (angling the target ~20 degrees) lets a tube:
options: Eliminate the need for cooling
Spread heat over a large area while presenting a small effective focal spot for sharpness
Increase the kVp without more voltage
Remove the need for a vacuum
questions.explanation: Angling the focal track spreads the intense heat over a larger physical area, yet the beam viewed along its axis sees a small effective focal spot, which minimizes geometric unsharpness.

questions.question: A tube's duty cycle is exceeded during a long series of exposures. The most likely consequence is:
options: The kVp drifts higher
The target overheats and can crack, damaging the tube
The beam becomes more penetrating
Nothing, as duty cycle is only advisory
questions.explanation: Since ~99% of input energy becomes heat, running past the rated on/off ratio overheats the target; the cure is to rest the tube within its duty cycle so heat can be carried away.

questions.question: Compared with a linear accelerator at the same energy, a betatron typically offers:
options: Much higher output (dose rate) and faster exposures
A smaller focal spot and sharper images, but lower output (longer exposures)
No need for shielding
Only low-energy output below 500 keV
questions.explanation: The betatron's small focal spot yields excellent sharpness on thick parts, but its dose rate is lower than a linac's, so it trades speed for definition.

questions.question: A high-energy accelerator's radiation output is most properly expressed in SI units as:
options: Curies (Ci)
Coulombs per kilogram of air (C/kg), often as a rate at one meter
Half-value layers (HVL)
Becquerels per second
questions.explanation: Exposure in SI is the coulomb per kilogram of air (replacing the roentgen); the curie and becquerel are units of activity, and HVL describes attenuation.

questions.question: In a fluoroscopic screen, increasing thickness and phosphor coarseness to raise conversion efficiency and brightness will:
options: Improve sharpness with no penalty
Worsen screen unsharpness because light spreads more before leaving the layer
Lower the X-ray energy
Eliminate the need for an image intensifier
questions.explanation: A thicker, coarser, brighter screen lets emitted light diffuse laterally, blurring fine detail; this speed-versus-definition trade is why critical work still favors film or high-resolution digital.
